currency.name: Cuban Peso
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 2,000 CUP (cash is king in Cuba)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Cuba on?
mapQuestions.answer: North America, in the Caribbean. Cuba is the biggest island in the Caribbean Sea, about 145 km south of Florida 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Cuba?
mapQuestions.answer: Havana, on the north coast, facing Florida. About 2 million people live in Havana itself, and it is famous for its colourful colonial buildings and classic American cars from the 1950s.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Cuba speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Spanish, with a fast, sing-song Caribbean accent. Many older Cubans also speak some Russian (a leftover from the Soviet era) and younger people increasingly learn English.

mapQuestions.question: Why are there so many 1950s American cars in Cuba?
mapQuestions.answer: Because of a long American trade ban (starting 1962) that meant Cuba couldn't import new American cars for decades. Cubans kept their 1950s Chevys, Cadillacs and Buicks running with hand-made parts, and now those old cars are a famous Cuban image.

mapQuestions.question: What does the Cuban flag mean?
mapQuestions.answer: Three blue stripes for the three original provinces, two white stripes for purity of independence, a red triangle for the blood of independence fighters, and one white star (the "Lone Star") for the country itself.

foods.name: Ropa vieja
foods.description: Shredded slow-cooked beef in a rich tomato, pepper and onion sauce, served with rice, black beans and fried plantain. Cuba's national dish.

foods.name: Moros y cristianos
foods.description: "Moors and Christians": black beans and rice cooked together so the rice turns dark grey. Eaten with almost every Cuban meal.

foods.name: Cuban sandwich
foods.description: Roast pork, ham, Swiss cheese, mustard and pickles in pressed Cuban bread. Invented by Cuban immigrants in Florida but adopted back in Cuba.

foods.name: Camarones al ajillo
foods.description: Prawns sautéed in olive oil with loads of garlic and a squeeze of lime. A Cuban coast dish.

foods.name: Tostones
foods.description: Twice-fried green plantain rounds, smashed flat and salty, served as a side with rice and beans. Crunchy outside, soft inside.

foods.name: Flan
foods.description: A wobbly caramel custard, served cold, often with a dollop of strong Cuban coffee on the side. Brought from Spain centuries ago, perfected in Cuba.

scavengerItems.label: A bright pink, turquoise or red 1950s American car still on the road
A salsa or son band playing live in a courtyard or bar
A pastel-painted colonial house in Havana, Trinidad or Cienfuegos
A small cup of strong "cafecito" (Cuban coffee) at a street counter
Royal palms swaying on a Cuban beach
A "tobacco-farm" valley in Viñales (UNESCO World Heritage)
A mural with images of Cuba's revolutionary history
The Capitolio building in Havana (looks just like the US Capitol)
Cuban ballet dancers practising (the National Ballet of Cuba is famous worldwide)
A Cuban tody, a tiny green-and-pink bird found only in Cuba
Dolphins off the coast of Maria la Gorda or the Bay of Pigs

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the north coast. Crumbling but stunning colonial old town (Habana Vieja), the Malecón seafront promenade, and 1950s cars rumbling past everywhere.
A perfectly preserved colonial town in central Cuba, with pastel houses, cobbled streets, and live salsa music in the main square every night.
A green valley in western Cuba with limestone "mogote" hills sticking up out of tobacco fields. UNESCO World Heritage. Old-style farming is everywhere.
A long white-sand beach resort strip on a peninsula east of Havana. The most famous beach in Cuba.
The second city, in the south-east. The cradle of son music, salsa and Cuban revolutionary history. Hotter, hillier and Caribbean-feeling.
"Bahía de Cochinos", a deep bay with some of the best snorkelling and diving in the Caribbean. Famous historically too, for the 1961 failed invasion.

senses.smell: Strong cigar tobacco from a Viñales farm, salty Atlantic air on the Malecón, sweet sugar-cane juice, garlic and lime in a Cuban kitchen, the petrol-smoke of old American cars…
senses.hear: Salsa and son music everywhere (especially in Trinidad and Santiago), Spanish flying fast and loud, "¿Qué bolá, asere?" between friends, the rumble of 1950s engines, waves slapping the Malecón sea wall…
senses.taste: Sweet milky cafecito coffee, tangy "ropa vieja" shredded beef, crispy salty tostones, smooth flan, fresh mango and guava fruit cut on the street…
senses.feel: Hot Caribbean sun, the sea breeze along the Malecón, soft white sand at Varadero, the sticky humidity of summer, the smooth leather of an old American car seat…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Cuba full of 1950s American cars?
stories.body: Before 1959, Cuba was one of the biggest markets for American cars: Chevrolets, Buicks, Cadillacs, Ford Fairlanes, all over the streets of Havana. Then in 1959 the Cuban Revolution brought Fidel Castro to power, and in 1962 the United States introduced a trade ban (an "embargo") that meant no new American cars (or parts) could be sold to Cuba. Cubans kept the 1950s cars they already had running, swapping in Russian Lada engines, hand-cut spare parts, and incredible mechanical creativity. Sixty-plus years later, those same cars are still rolling, gleamingly painted, mostly used as taxis for tourists. They have become one of Cuba's most famous images.

stories.question: Where does salsa come from?
stories.body: Salsa as we know it today grew out of "son cubano", a music style that started in the hills around Santiago de Cuba in the late 1800s. Son mixed Spanish guitar with African drumming brought by enslaved peoples to the Caribbean. By the 1930s, Cuban son bands had spread to New York, where they mixed with jazz and gave birth to "mambo", then "cha-cha", and finally "salsa" in the 1960s and 70s. Today salsa is played in every Cuban town, every night, and Cubans grow up dancing it almost before they can walk. The 1999 documentary "Buena Vista Social Club" introduced old Cuban son musicians to the wider world and started a huge revival.

stories.question: Why are the Cuban tobacco fields so famous?
stories.body: Cuba grows what most cigar experts agree are the best tobacco leaves on Earth, mostly in the western Pinar del Río province around Viñales. The mix of red soil, humidity and altitude is supposedly unique. Tobacco is farmed by small families using ox-and-plough techniques that have barely changed in 150 years. The leaves are dried in tall thatched barns, fermented for months, then sorted, rolled and aged into cigars. The most famous brands (Cohiba, Montecristo, Romeo y Julieta) are still hand-rolled in Havana factories where each roller is expected to make 80 to 100 cigars a day. Tobacco is one of Cuba's biggest exports.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A country can become famous for one specific thing because of the soil and climate. What does your home country make better than anywhere else?

stories.question: Why is Cuba so different from its Caribbean neighbours?
stories.body: In 1959 a young lawyer called Fidel Castro and a group of guerrilla fighters (including Che Guevara) overthrew the Cuban government in the Cuban Revolution. They turned Cuba into a one-party Communist state and aligned closely with the Soviet Union. The United States, just 145 km away, opposed all of this fiercely, leading to the trade ban that has shaped Cuban life ever since: no McDonald's, no Starbucks, no big American chains, no big American cars, no easy access to imports. Cuba developed in its own direction: very high literacy rates, free healthcare and education, but a much smaller economy than its neighbours and many shortages. It is unique, and walking through Havana feels like walking into a parallel-history Caribbean.

stories.question: What is the Malecón?
stories.body: The Malecón is the 8-kilometre seafront promenade that curves along the north edge of Havana. It is part road, part sea wall, part open-air social space. Every evening, Cubans of all ages gather on it: families with picnic chairs, teenagers with guitars, lovers staring out to sea, fishermen casting lines, kids running in the spray when big waves crash over the wall. There is no entry fee, no booking, no rules. The Malecón has been the unofficial living-room of Havana for over a hundred years. The waves on stormy nights are spectacular, sometimes washing across the road and into the apartments.

convoQuestions.question: Have you tried "qué bolá, asere?" to anyone?
Havana, Trinidad, beach, tobacco country, which has felt the most "Cuba"?
Have you ridden in a 1950s American car? What did it feel like?
How does Cuban music change the mood of a place?
Cuba developed in its own direction for 60 years. What does that show about how a country can shape itself?

wordSearch.hint: Cuban foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Cuban tody
animals.description: A tiny bright-green bird with a pink belly. Endemic to Cuba (found nowhere else). About 11 cm long.

animals.name: Cuban rock iguana
animals.description: On the cays and small islands off Cuba. Up to 1.5 m long, slow and harmless.

animals.name: Land crab
animals.description: In spring, millions of red land crabs migrate from the forest to the sea to lay eggs, especially around the Bay of Pigs.

animals.name: Cuban crocodile
animals.description: A small, agile crocodile species found only in Cuba's Zapata swamp. Critically endangered.

animals.name: Atlantic spotted dolphin
animals.description: Off Cuba's north coast, often playing in the bow waves of boats.

animals.name: Caribbean flamingo
animals.description: Big pink flocks in the Cayo Coco salt lagoons on the north coast.

animals.name: Bee hummingbird
animals.description: The smallest bird on Earth, only 5 cm long. Found only in Cuba.
